startScreen.startScreenSubtitle: A 40-year-old man presents with severe low back pain radiating to the leg. Apply your knowledge of vertebral column anatomy, intervertebral discs, and lumbar puncture landmarks to guide diagnosis and management.

endScreens.endScreenSubtitle: You correctly applied vertebral column anatomy including disc prolapse levels, lumbar puncture landmarks, and spinal pathology to manage this case.
You showed some knowledge but missed key concepts. Review intervertebral disc anatomy, common prolapse levels, and lumbar puncture technique.
Review vertebral column fundamentals: curvatures, disc anatomy, lumbar puncture landmarks, and common spinal pathologies (disc prolapse, Pott's disease, spondylolisthesis).

params.text:
Case Presentation

A 40-year-old office worker presents with sudden-onset severe low back pain after lifting a heavy box. The pain radiates down the left leg to the foot, worsened by coughing and straining. He has numbness in the lateral aspect of the left foot.

Examination findings:

  • Positive straight leg raise test at 30 degrees on the left
  • Diminished ankle reflex on the left
  • Weakness of left ankle plantarflexion
  • Reduced sensation over the lateral foot (S1 dermatome)

Key anatomy: The vertebral column has four curvatures: cervical lordosis, thoracic kyphosis, lumbar lordosis, and sacral kyphosis. The intervertebral disc consists of a central nucleus pulposus surrounded by the annulus fibrosus.

branchingQuestion.question: The patient has S1 radiculopathy (ankle reflex loss, plantarflexion weakness, lateral foot numbness). The L4-L5 and L5-S1 discs account for 90% of symptomatic disc prolapses. Which disc level is most likely affected?

alternatives.text: L5-S1 disc prolapse compressing the S1 nerve root

feedback.title: Correct!
feedback.subtitle: A posterolateral L5-S1 disc prolapse compresses the S1 nerve root (which exits below the S1 pedicle). S1 radiculopathy causes: absent ankle jerk, weakness of plantarflexion (gastrocnemius/soleus), and numbness in the S1 dermatome (lateral foot, sole, posterior calf). The L4-L5 and L5-S1 levels account for ~90% of symptomatic disc herniations.

alternatives.text: L3-L4 disc prolapse compressing the L4 nerve root

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: L4 radiculopathy would cause diminished knee jerk (not ankle jerk), weakness of ankle dorsiflexion and knee extension, and numbness in the medial leg — not the lateral foot. The clinical findings here (lost ankle reflex, weak plantarflexion, lateral foot numbness) point to S1 involvement from an L5-S1 disc prolapse.

alternatives.text: L4-L5 disc prolapse compressing the L5 nerve root

feedback.title: Close, but not the best answer
feedback.subtitle: L5 radiculopathy causes weakness of great toe extension (EHL) and ankle dorsiflexion, with numbness on the dorsum of the foot — but the ankle reflex is preserved. This patient has a lost ankle reflex and weak plantarflexion, which is S1, not L5. The L5-S1 disc is compressing the S1 root.

params.text:
Lumbar Puncture Considerations

The patient is stable and improving with conservative management. However, if a diagnostic lumbar puncture were needed, understanding the landmarks is critical.

Lumbar puncture anatomy:

  • The spinal cord ends at L1-L2 (conus medullaris) in adults
  • The needle is inserted at the L3-L4 or L4-L5 interspace — below the cord termination
  • The intercristal line (connecting the iliac crests) marks the L4 spinous process or L4-L5 interspace
  • The needle passes through: skin → subcutaneous tissue → supraspinous ligament → interspinous ligament → ligamentum flavum (produces a characteristic 'pop') → epidural space → dura → arachnoid → subarachnoid space

branchingQuestion.question: Why is lumbar puncture performed at L3-L4 or L4-L5, and not at a higher level?

alternatives.text: Because the spinal cord ends at L1-L2 — puncture below this level enters the cauda equina (nerve roots floating in CSF), avoiding cord damage

feedback.title: Correct!
feedback.subtitle: The spinal cord (conus medullaris) terminates at L1-L2 in adults. Below this level, only the cauda equina (nerve roots) and filum terminale are present, floating in CSF. A needle at L3-L4 or L4-L5 can safely enter the subarachnoid space to sample CSF without risking spinal cord injury. The nerve roots are pushed aside by the needle rather than being damaged.

alternatives.text: Because the interspinous spaces are wider at L3-L4, making needle insertion technically easier

feedback.title: Partially correct but missing the key reason
feedback.subtitle: While the lumbar interspinous spaces are relatively wide (especially in flexion), the primary reason for choosing L3-L4 or below is safety — the spinal cord ends at L1-L2. Puncture above this level risks cord injury. The wider spaces are a secondary advantage, not the primary indication for this level.

alternatives.text: Because CSF is only present in the lumbar subarachnoid space, not at higher levels

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: CSF fills the entire subarachnoid space from the brain to approximately S2. It is present at all spinal levels. The reason for choosing L3-L4 or L4-L5 is that the spinal cord ends at L1-L2 — puncture below this level avoids cord damage while still accessing CSF in the lumbar cistern.

params.text:
Key Learning Points

  • Vertebral curvatures: Four in sagittal plane — cervical lordosis, thoracic kyphosis, lumbar lordosis, sacral kyphosis; primary (thoracic/sacral) present at birth
  • Intervertebral disc: Central nucleus pulposus + surrounding annulus fibrosus; L4-L5 and L5-S1 account for 90% of symptomatic prolapses
  • Lumbar puncture: L3-L4 or L4-L5 interspace; cord ends at L1-L2; ligamentum flavum produces the 'pop'
  • Spondylolisthesis: Anterior slipping of one vertebra over another, most commonly L4 over L5
  • Pott's disease: Tuberculosis of the spine — most common cause of spinal cord compression in India
  • Cervical vertebrae: Unique transverse foramina transmitting vertebral arteries
  • Sacroiliac joint: Affected in ankylosing spondylitis (HLA-B27 associated)
